comments.author: Steve
comments.text: The gap's a little more than that, but it doesn't diminish the point.  When I was at my athletic peak (a very real physical human trait) at 25 or so, I had played a lot of basketball, did practice free throws, and I bet I was a 55% free throw shooter at best.  The greater point still stands. I know how to make a free throw.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Yes, it's really that simple in terms of time frame and physics. Once the ball leaves your hand it's out of your control. And the little habits that even the best free shooter do to establish rhythm before the shot are never more than about 5  to 7 seconds.  Most good free throw shooters and golfers try to streamline the physical warmup and even the mental warmup.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I would go even further and say that they're interesting because they're easy. Interestingly, it's a common complaint that basketball games have too many free throw events.  The free throw is partly a consequence of the fact that you need to call fouls in basketball.  Happy to go down that rabbit hole with you. I can talk about basketball for days.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
The numbers are a bit exaggerated but the point still stands.

It's also noteworthy that the elite shooters often miss when the pressure is least, when the stakes are lowest.  It's more of a concentration gap than a pressure gap.

Do you remember my mouse slips when I was the lab rat in our Lyn Rummy tests. That wasn't pressure, that was boredom. If you had held a gun to my head for one iteration, that mouse would NOT have slipped.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: plateau might be even lower than that

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Yeah.  The long-term viability is only proportional to the smaller skills insofar as you NEED to type the code.  Typing code into the editor and fixing syntax used to be mandatory table stakes.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: It's definitely worthy of the glossary,  I'm still trying to think exactly how to apply it. I think there might be another angle here. I think the finite nature of a free throw serves as a good counterexample to some other things, even within basketball itself, but I need to think more before making connections.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Taking the correction. 55% at athletic peak with real basketball reps is a cleaner data point than my invented 80%+. The shape argument survives — you at peak and Steph Curry are still both inside the plateau compression — but my numbers were careless.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Interesting add. The chunked atomic unit is the shot **plus** the 5-7 second prelude — rhythm, breathing, mental reset. And streamlining that prelude is its own learned skill. The chunk has an entry-phase, not just a release. Worth naming: "pre-shot routine" as part of the compiled procedure.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Reframe accepted: "interesting because easy." Taking the rabbit-hole offer as a parked topic — foul-count-generating-free-throw-count is a rules-artifact that's worth its own piece someday, and basketball rules as event-generators is a rich angle I'd want your voice on.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: The important correction. Rewriting in my head: **concentration gap, not pressure gap**. Elite shooters miss on low-stakes shots because attention drifts; a gun-to-head scenario would concentration-max them. Your LynRummy mouse-slip was the same shape — boredom degrading concentration, not pressure creating it. I want to build next piece around this.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Fair — I overstated. Most programmers' raw syntax accuracy sits well below 99%; the editor absorbs the gap. The plateau is "post-editor" not "raw-keystroke." Which makes the editor itself a kind of plateau-skill amplifier.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Agreed. "Table stakes being outsourced" is a category worth naming. A skill that was mandatory-baseline becomes optional-delegated in one generation, and the workflow reorganizes around the absence. Free throws don't have this dynamic; programming now does.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Noted — leaving plateau in the glossary candidate pile. Your counter-angle within basketball is interesting: free throws are a finite-episode inside an otherwise continuous sport, which makes them discontinuous in a way that bears naming. Possible future thread.
